coredata.dc:title: Vertically aligned rutile TiO2 nanorods sensitized with sulfur and nitrogen co-doped graphene quantum dots for water splitting: An energy level study
coredata.dc:creator: Bayat, A.
Saievar-Iranizad, E.
coredata.dc:description: Highly S and N co-doped graphene quantum dots (SNGQDs) with an average width of ∼4 nm and a broad absorption band in the visible region of light were investigated as an effective light absorber, and their LUMO and HOMO energy states against the vacuum were determined using cyclic voltammetry for the first time. Vertically deposited TiO2 nanorods films were decorated with various amounts of the SNGQD solution (x = 5, 10, 20 and 30 μL) by a spin coater and their photovoltaic properties were comprehensively investigated for water splitting applications. Optical characterizations reveal that the SNGQDs are highly photoluminescent and absorb UV and visible photons with wavelengths up to 700 nm. The prepared SNGQDs–x/TiO2 electrode shows a photocurrent enhancement of ∼4.5× as compared to the bare TiO2 electrode for the optimum sample (x = 20 μL) at an applied voltage of P = 0.5 V using 1 M Na2SO4 solution as the electrolyte.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Thiourea
Cyclic voltammetry
Photoelectrochemical
Hydrothermal
Quantum confinement

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Decoration of TiO2 NRs with SNGQDs
Generation mechanism of H2 and O2 molecules under illumination

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Formation mechanism of (A) TiO2 NRs, (B) SNGQDs solution and (C) decorating of TiO2 NRs with the SNGQDs.

(a) Top-view and (b) tilted angle FESEM images of the TiO2 NRs vertically grown on the FTO substrate, (c) TEM image of the SNGQDs and (d) TEM image of a TiO2 nanorod decorated with SNGQDs and arrows show the SNGQDs.
(a) FTIR spectrum of the obtained SNGQDs, (b) Raman spectra of the SNGQDs/TiO2 NRs and bare TiO2 NRs.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the SNGQDs solution and inset shows the photograph of SNGQDs solution schematically under excitation wavelengths of 310, 390 and 590 nm, respectively, (b) Three emission peaks observed in the PL spectra at the excitation wavelengths of 310, 390, and 590 nm, respectively. (c) Tauc plots of the SNGQDs showing three linear regions.

(a) Cyclic voltammetry of the SNGQDs/Glassy carbon and bare Glassy carbon, (b) Energy levels of the SNGQDs related to doping of oxygen, nitrogen and sulfur atoms and arrows are obtained from Tauc plots and PL spectra that are in good agreement with cyclic voltammetry measurement. (C) Convenient energy states of rutile TiO2 and SNGQDs against reduction energy level of hydrogen and oxidation energy level of water leading to generation of O2 and H2 molecules under illumination.
(a) Schematic illustration of H2 and O2 generation under illumination.

J-V curves under illumination (100 mW/cm2) versus Ag/AgCl at: (a) constant bias voltage (0.5 V), inset shows equivalent circuit, and (b) various bias voltages, (c) Nyquist plots for the SNGQDs–x/TiO2 NRs and bare TiO2 NRs photoanodes.
A summary of the measured photoelectrochemical parameters for SNGQDs–x/TiO2 NRs electrodes.

head.ce:title: Vertically aligned rutile TiO2 nanorods sensitized with sulfur and nitrogen co-doped graphene quantum dots for water splitting: An energy level study

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Faculty of Basic Science, Tarbiat Modares University, P.O.Box: 14115-111, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly S and N co-doped graphene quantum dots (SNGQDs) with an average width of ∼4 nm and a broad absorption band in the visible region of light were investigated as an effective light absorber, and their LUMO and HOMO energy states against the vacuum were determined using cyclic voltammetry for the first time. Vertically deposited TiO2 nanorods films were decorated with various amounts of the SNGQD solution (x = 5, 10, 20 and 30 μL) by a spin coater and their photovoltaic properties were comprehensively investigated for water splitting applications. Optical characterizations reveal that the SNGQDs are highly photoluminescent and absorb UV and visible photons with wavelengths up to 700 nm. The prepared SNGQDs–x/TiO2 electrode shows a photocurrent enhancement of ∼4.5× as compared to the bare TiO2 electrode for the optimum sample (x = 20 μL) at an applied voltage of P = 0.5 V using 1 M Na2SO4 solution as the electrolyte.

ce:section.ce:para: Splitting water into hydrogen and oxygen via photoelectrochemical cells would be an environmentally-friendly way to produce clean and renewable energy [1–5]. There are some important requirements affecting the selection of a semiconductor as a photoanode for water splitting applications: (1) the semiconductor's valence band must be lower than the oxidation state of water, (2) its conduction band must be higher than the reduction state of hydrogen, (3) its band gap energy must be theoretically wider than 1.23 eV (in practice, because of overpotentials, it must be bigger than ∼1.7 eV) [6], (4) its absorption in the visible region should be high, and (5) must be abundant and stable. To the best of our knowledge, there is no naturally occurring semiconductor that can fulfill all the aforementioned requirements. Therefore, various methods have been offered to satisfy the necessary conditions. Doping semiconductors with interstitial atoms like N [7], doping with metal or non-metal elements [8,9], and annealing in hydrogen [10] have provided limited success in extending their absorption range. Another common approach relies on the sensitization of them with narrow bandgap chalcogenide materials (CdS, PbS, CdSe, etc) [11,12] which pose environmental problems and exhibit a fast photocorrosion in aqueous solutions.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) show a great promise in a wide range of applications, but they have a weak light absorption in the visible region of light, so they are not a good sensitizer for water splitting applications [13,14]. To effectively tune the absorption region of GQDs, a promising approach is to doping GQDs with heteroatoms such as N- and S-doped graphene quantum dots. Due to their unique properties, they are a good candidate for sensitizing and improving the properties of base semiconductors [15,16]. Various researchers have synthesized and investigated doped graphene quantum dots and studied their properties [17,18], whereas there are very rare studies on the energy levels of heteroatom-doped graphene quantum dots.

In this work, we used highly S and N co-doped graphene quantum dots (SNGQD) with a broad absorption band (ranging from ultraviolet to red) as an effective light sensitizer and determined the highest occupied molecular-orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular-orbital (LUMO) states of the SNGQDs for water splitting applications. To show photoelectrochemical effects of the SNGQDs on the semiconductors, vertically grown rutile TiO2 nanorods (NRs) on fluorine tin oxide (FTO) glass were used as a base semiconductor. Identifying the underlying mechanism of SNGQDs under photoirradiation in conjunction with metal oxides could be informative to promote their utilization in solar or fuel generation processes.
All materials and chemical reagents were purchased from Merck Co (Germany) without any further purification, as well as the autoclave was purchased from shimaz Co (Iran).

SNGQDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method adapted from the literature [19] with some effective modifications in the volume and concentration to obtain highly S and N co-doped GQDs. Briefly; 1 g of citric acid and 2 g of thiourea were dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water. The mixture was then treated by a one-step hydrothermal method in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (75 mL) at 160 °C for 4 h, producing SNGQDs' dispersions with a good solubility in water after filtration with a filter paper. The initial liquid sample was transparent (colorless) and it changed to green after the synthesis of SNGQDs.

TiO2 NRs were grown vertically on fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO, F:SnO2) glasses using a hydrothermal method without TiO2 seed layer. In a typical synthesis, 12 mL of deionized water was mixed with 6 mL of concentrated hydrochloric acid to reach a total volume of 18 mL in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (75 mL volume). After addition of 0.3 mL of Titanium Isopropoxide (TTIP), the mixture was stirred under ambient conditions for 5 min. After that, the pieces of FTO substrates (F:SnO2, 1 × 1.5 cm2) were ultrasonically cleaned for 15 min in deionized water and acetone and were put into the autoclave. The hydrothermal synthesis was conducted at 160 °C for 7.5 h in an oven. After the synthesis, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature naturally. The obtained samples were washed with deionized water and dried at 80 °C in air.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Decoration of TiO2 NRs with SNGQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To sensitize TiO2 NRs with the SNGQDs, various amounts of the SNGQDs solution (5, 10, 20 and 30 μL, with the concentration of 50 g/L) were dropped on the TiO2 NRs substrates using a spin coater with a rotation rate and deposition time of 3000 rpm and 3 min, respectively (the formation mechanism is schematically shown in Fig. 1 ). Then, to achieve a better adherence of SNGQDs on the TiO2 NRs surfaces, the SNGQDs/TiO2 NRs electrodes were heated for 30 min in an oven at 150 °C.

Photoluminescence (PL) emission was recorded using a PL spectrometer (Gilden photonics) and optical absorbance measurements were carried out using a spectrophotometer (Unico 4802). The structural morphology of the samples was studied using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) and a transmission electron microscope (TEM, CM30 300 kV). Surface topography of the particles on a mica substrate was studied by an atomic force microscope (AFM) using a commercial microscope (Veeco Autoprobe CP-research) in air with a 10 nm radius of curvature. Steady state current density voltage (J-V) and EIS measurements were carried out using a VSP-300 Multichannel Potentiostat/Galvanostat EIS (Bio-Logic Science Instruments). X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD, Co-Kα radiation source, Philips, X'Pert MPD) were studied in order to investigate the structures and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was employed using a FTIR spectrometer (Nicolet Nexus 670). Raman analysis (BRUKER, Germany, SENTERRA) was used to prove the appearance of SNGQDs on TiO2 NRs surfaces. For J-V and EIS measurements, three electrode configurations were used, where TiO2 NRs and SNGQDs/TiO2 NRs photo-electrodes were connected to the working electrode, and a saturated Ag/AgCl was used as the reference electrode and a Pt plate was connected to the counter electrode. The solution of Na2SO4 (1 M, pH 6) was used as the electrolyte, and the electrodes were illuminated using a xenon lamp and the light intensity was adjusted with a thermopile to 100 mW/cm2.

Fig. 2 (a and b) shows typical top view and tilted FESEM images of the TiO2 NRs films vertically grown at 160 °C for 7.5 h. According to this figure, TiO2 NRs have been formed with an average diameter and height of about 120 nm and 1 μm, respectively. Fig. 2-c shows a TEM image of the obtained SNGQDs, indicating that they are uniformly formed with the average size of ∼4 nm and the inset shows an HRTEM image of SNGQDs. Fig. 2-d shows a TEM image of a TiO2 nanorod decorated with the SNGQDs. To further confirm the morphology and thickness of the as-formed SNGQDs, AFM topography images were analyzed. The SNGQDs solution was drop casted on a mica substrate. Then, AFM images were taken from various parts of the sample surface. According to Fig. S1, the SNGQDs particles are very small, consistent with the TEM image. The height profile of the SNGQDs on a mica substrate is shown in the inset of Fig. S1.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was used to characterize the functional groups of the prepared SNGQDs. Under the hydrothermal process, citric acid is dehydrated to form CC bonds, which are the elementary unit of SNGQDs. As can be seen in Fig. 3 -a, the following peaks are observed: the broad absorption bands in the range of 3000–3500 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of N–H and O–H. The peaks indicate that there are many amino and hydroxyl groups and these functional groups improve the hydrophilicity and stability of SNGQDs in aqueous systems. Bands at 1610 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 are due to the bending vibrations of CC and C–N bonds, respectively. While, peaks at 1240 cm−1 in SNGQDs are attributed to the CS, and a weak C–S stretching band was observed at 635 cm−1. The peak around 1022 cm−1 corresponds to the C–O–C bond and the peak around 1730 cm−1 is ascribed to the CO group. The peak located at 1564 cm−1, corresponds to the CN bond, while the triple bond of carbon-carbon shows a peak at 2050-2260 cm−1. All peaks in the FTIR spectrum are in agreement with the SNGQDs peaks [18,20].

Raman spectroscopy was employed to confirm the formation of TiO2 rutile phase and the existence of SNGQDs on TiO2 NRs surfaces. Raman spectra of TiO2 NRs and SNGQDs/TiO2 NRs thin films are shown in Fig. 3-b. The spectrum of TiO2 NRs reveals two Raman peaks at 450 and 620 cm−1, which were assigned to Eg and A1g modes, respectively. These peaks could be well attributed to the rutile phase of TiO2 [21,22]. Furthermore, XRD patterns of the TiO2 NRs are shown in Fig. S2, proving that the rutile phase is formed properly. Two additional peaks at about 1354 and 1600 cm−1 for graphene structure are observed in the Raman spectrum for the SNGQDs/TiO2 NRs, which can be assigned to the disordered sp2 carbon (D-band) and well-ordered graphite (G-band) [23,24], respectively. The Raman spectra confirm the incorporation of SNGQDs on the surfaces of TiO2 NRs.

As mentioned above, to assay the SNGQDs as a light sensitizer for water splitting, UV–Vis and cyclic voltammetry were used and their HOMO and LUMO band states were determined and photoelectrochemical measurements were performed.

UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the SNGQDs solution is shown in Fig. 4 -a. The UV–Vis spectrum displays absorption bands centered at 303, 401, and 580 nm, which are similar to those of other work [19]. However, in our work the intensity of the peak at 580 nm is very higher than those of the other two peaks, showing that doping with S atoms is high. The origin of these peaks is related to the electron transition in heteroatom-containing GQDs. The origin of the absorption peak at 303 nm is attributed to the n→π* electron transition in CO groups. The absorption peak at 401 nm corresponds to the n→π* transition of the conjugated CN, and the absorption peak at 580 nm originates from the n→π* transition of the conjugated CS bond [19].

To investigate photoluminescence properties, SNGQDs solution was excited at wavelengths from 350 to 620 nm with an interval of 10 nm. The corresponding overall PL spectra are shown in Fig. S3. In the PL spectra, the as-prepared SNGQDs exhibit three excitation wavelength-independent photoluminescence (PL) regions with emission peaks at 430 (blue), 480 (bluish green), and 633 nm (red) for excitation wavelengths of 310, 390 and 590 nm, respectively, as shown in Fig. 4-b.

By plotting the square of the absorption energy (αhv, where α is the absorbance and hv is the photon energy) against hv (known as Tauc plot [25]), we obtained three linear regions (Fig. 3-c) that show the SNGQDs solution has three absorption edges originating from doping with S, N, and O atoms. By extrapolating these regions, three energy values were obtained (3.4, 2.78 and 1.9 eV), and the minimum of them is the band gap (1.9 eV).

As these small SNGQDs particles show a broad absorption in the visible region of light with three absorption edges, they could be employed as a powerful energy harvester for photocatalysts in environment and energy application. As mentioned earlier, positions of HOMO and LUMO energy levels of a quantum dot against the oxidation state of water and the reduction state of hydrogen are very important as a criterion to employ quantum dots as an effective light sensitizer for water splitting application. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were used to directly determine the LUMO and HOMO energy levels of the SNGQDs, and hence to find the bandgap energy.

Applying a potential higher the LUMO level to form an accumulation layer, or below the HOMO to form an inversion layer, can lead to the abrupt emergence of cathodic and anodic currents, respectively. The electrode for electrochemical analysis was prepared by drop-casting SNGQDs suspensions (5 μL) and nafion (0.5 μL, 5% w) onto a glassy carbon electrode (with a diameter of 3 mm) and dried naturally at room temperature. Cyclic voltammetry measurements were performed with a three-electrode configuration, i.e. SNGQDs/glassy carbon as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode and a Pt plate as the counter electrode, in an acetonitrile solution including 0.1 M Tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate (TBAPF6). Fig. 5 -a depicts the behavior of the glassy carbon electrode in the absence and presence of the SNGQDs. The oxidation and reduction peaks related to electron and hole injections into the conduction and valence bands were consequently used to determine the EHOMO and ELUMO levels using the following three equations [26,27]:
E
HOMO
= − e
( 4.75 +
E
Ox , vs
NHE
)
( eV )
E
LUMO
= − e
( 4.75 +
E
Red , vs
NHE
)
( eV )
E
NHE
=
E
Ag / AgCl
+ 0.197

The SNGQDs/glassy carbon electrode shows a reduction peak at −0.7 V and three oxidation peaks at +1.15,+1.81 and 2.45 V vs Ag/AgCl as the reference. The values of ELUMO and EHOMO calculated from these peaks are −4.25 and −6.1 eV, respectively. The band-gap energy determined from the cyclic voltammetry was 1.85 eV, which is in good agreement with that obtained from the optical absorption spectrum (1.9 eV). The Cyclic voltammetry measurements, HOMO and LUMO energy levels of the SNGQDs, the oxidation state of water, the reduction state of hydrogen, as well as the conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) of rutile TiO2 are shown in Fig. 5.

Thus, as proved based on UV–Vis and CV measurements, the obtained SNGQDs fulfill all the requirements mentioned in introduction. The LUMO (equivalent to the conduction band) energy level of SNGQDs is higher than the reduction energy state of hydrogen, HOMO (equivalent to the valance band) energy level is lower than the oxidation state of water, band gap of the SNGQDs is bigger than ∼1.7 eV, and SNGQDs have a broad absorption region from ultraviolet to red wavelengths, as well as the precursor materials and methods are inexpensive for application in photoelectrochemical cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Generation mechanism of H2 and O2 molecules under illumination
ce:section.ce:para: To generate H2 and O2 molecules, the position of conduction and valance bands of the semiconductor should fit into the potential window of water reduction and oxidation potentials [28–30], respectively. Both rutile TiO2 and SNGQDs have convenient positions of conduction and valance bands (CB and VB) for water splitting (as shown in Fig. 5). When light shines on the SNGQDs/TiO2 NRs photoanode, SNGQDs particles absorb photons and then electrons in the HOMO of SNGQDs are elevated to the LUMO, leaving holes behind; then the excited electrons move from LUMO to the conduction band of TiO2 NRs and then gathered using a charge collector and transferred to the counter electrode (a Pt plate) with an external contact. Simultaneously, according to the band positions, holes are injected into the solution and oxidize the H2O molecules, leading to the formation of H+ and O–H. Again, holes oxidize O–H, leading to the formation of O2 on the photoanode electrode and protons (H+) move to the counter electrode through the electrolyte and reduces to form H2 molecules. To show photoelectrochemical properties of the SNGQDs, photocurrent density and EIS measurements under illumination were performed and the equivalent circuit for EIS measurements was fitted on Randles circuit. The electron-hole generation at the SNGQDs, the transmission of electrons into TiO2 NRs and the generation of O2 and H2 are schematically explained in Fig. 6 .

Fig. 7 (a and b) shows the measured photocurrent density for the SNGQDs–x/TiO2 NRs under a sudden on and off switching (at a constant bias voltage of 0.5 V vs. Ag/AgCl electrode) and as a function of applied bias voltages, ranging from −0.2–0.9 V, respectively, in which x = 5, 10, 20 and 30 μL with the concentration of 50 g/L. Under illumination, the resulting photoinduced charge accumulation at SNGQDs is believed to be the reason for the significantly increased current density. All of the SNGQDs–x/TiO2 NRs composites exhibit a higher photocurrent density than the bare TiO2 NRs. However, at the applied voltage of 0.5 V, photocurrent densities of 259, 468, 853 and 611 μA cm−2 were obtained for samples with x = 5, 10, 20 and 30 μL, respectively. For the bare TiO2 NRs the amount of 188 μA cm−2 was obtained. The optimum value of SNGQDs loading was determined 20 μL, which shows a ∼4.5× higher photocatalytic activity than the bare TiO2 NRs (at the applied voltage of 0.5 V). The photocurrent generation of the SNGQDs–x/TiO2 composites increases by increasing the amount of the added SNGQDs from 5 to 20 μL. Further increasing x from 20 to 30 leads to a less effective connection between surface excited electrons, and electron–hole recombination is more probable due to the electron injection process being slow down, leading to a decrease in the photocurrent density of the SNGQD–x/TiO2 NRs photoanode. In other words, when TiO2 NRs are covered with an excess amount of SNGQDs, the effective surface area participating in reduction/oxidation processes reduces, and the probability of the photo-generated e−–h+ recombination increases, resulting in a lower Jph value. A similar behavior was also observed for CdS/CdSe [31] and Fe2O3/TiO2 [32] nanotube systems. These two positive and negative sides, resulting from the addition of SNGQDs, result in an optimum amount of x = 20 μL leading to the highest current density. The effect of increasing x can be understood from the electrochemical impedance results.

As shown in Fig. 7-c, the typical EIS for the bare TiO2 NRs and SNGQDs–x/TiO2 NRs electrodes were presented as Nyquist plots under illumination, where the horizontal axis is the real part (Z1) and the vertical axis is the imaginary part (Z2) of the sample impedance [33]. Under illumination, the semi-circle of the SNGQDs-x/TiO2 NRs samples is smaller than that of the bare TiO2 NRs, indicating a lower charge transfer resistance. Charge transfer resistance (Rct) for the bare TiO2 NRs and SNGQDs–x/TiO2 NRs systems were obtained and listed in Table 1 . It is well known that a lower Rct is in favor of a higher photocurrent production, which is in a good agreement with our J–V curves described above. These results suggest the effective separation of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs and the fast interfacial charge transfer in the prepared SNGQD–x/TiO2 NRs composites. Hence, a higher rate of the photocatalytic activity of SNGQD–x/TiO2 composites would be achieved.

By analyzing UV–Vis spectra, we showed that the S and N co-doped graphene quantum dots have a broad absorption band with three absorption edges, and by determining LUMO and HOMO energy levels of the SNGQDs, we proved that the LUMO energy state of the SNGQDs is higher than the reduction state of hydrogen and the HOMO energy state is lower than the oxidation state of water. Thus, the SNGQDs are an effective light sensitizer for water splitting application and this method can be applied to other quantum dots.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank Research Council of the Tarbiat Modares University for financial supports.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jallcom.2018.05.008.
